summary:
The Edict of Thessalonica (Cunctos Populos, 380 AD), issued by Emperor Theodosius I, was the decree that made Nicene Christianity the sole official state religion of the [[event:81|Roman Empire]] — the culmination of the empire's Christianization that had begun with [[event:2857|Constantine's conversion]] and the [[event:2028|Edict of Milan]]. The edict declared that all subjects of the empire were to follow the Nicene Creed as defined at the [[event:2029|Council of Nicaea]], condemned all other religious practices (pagan and heterodox Christian alike) as criminal, and authorized the persecution of pagans and heretics. All public pagan worship was banned; pagan temples were closed or converted to churches; pagan sacrifices were prohibited under penalty of death.

The edict's consequences were profound and long-lasting. It completed the legal transformation of the empire from a state that tolerated multiple religions to a confessional Christian state — the template for the 'Christendom' concept that would define medieval European civilization. The closure of pagan temples and the persecution of the last pagan intellectuals (including the murder of the philosopher Hypatia in Alexandria in 415 AD by a Christian mob) accelerated the loss of classical pagan culture. The great Serapeum temple complex in Alexandria was destroyed in 391 AD. The Olympic Games, held continuously since 776 BC, were abolished as a pagan festival. The [[event:2103|Neoplatonic Academy]] in Athens was closed by Justinian in 529 AD. The edict thus marks not only the Christian triumph but the beginning of what critics would call the suppression of classical culture in the name of religious orthodoxy.
